questions.text: How well can you explain how context window limits affect multi-file tasks in Claude Code?

choices.label: Never — I don't know what a context window is
Sometimes — I have a rough idea but can't explain it clearly
Reliably — I can explain token limits and their practical effects
Confidently — I predict context constraints and adapt my approach based on task size and session state

questions.explanation: Understanding context window limits is foundational. Practitioners who cannot explain this concept will struggle to manage long sessions or multi-file tasks effectively.

questions.text: How well do you understand the difference between Claude Code's tool use (running bash commands, editing files) and direct text generation?

choices.label: Never — I don't distinguish between them
Sometimes — I notice when tools are used but don't fully understand why
Reliably — I understand which actions use tools and which are generated text
Fluently — I predict tool use patterns and leverage them deliberately to shape outcomes

questions.explanation: Distinguishing tool use from generation helps practitioners understand what Claude Code is actually doing, which is critical for diagnosing unexpected behavior.

questions.text: Can you describe what happens when Claude Code executes a bash command — including how it decides to run the command, what permissions apply, and how the output is processed?

choices.label: Never — I have no idea what happens behind the scenes
Sometimes — I know it runs commands but not the details
Reliably — I understand the permission model and output flow
Confidently — I can explain the full lifecycle and use that understanding to debug unexpected behavior

questions.explanation: Understanding the command execution lifecycle helps practitioners predict behavior, configure permissions appropriately, and debug issues when commands fail.

questions.text: How often do you provide context about the codebase (file paths, conventions, architecture) before asking Claude Code to make changes?

choices.label: Never — I jump straight to the request
Sometimes — when I remember or the task seems complex
Reliably — I set context for most non-trivial tasks
Deliberately — I gauge how much context is needed per task, providing thorough setup for complex changes and minimal context when the codebase speaks for itself

questions.explanation: Context-setting is the single most important factor in prompt quality. Without it, Claude Code operates with incomplete information and produces less accurate results.

questions.text: How effectively can you decompose a large feature into a sequence of well-scoped prompts for Claude Code?

choices.label: Never — I use a single prompt for everything
Sometimes — I split into a couple of prompts but struggle with ordering
Reliably — I plan 3-5 sequential prompts with clear dependencies
Fluently — I design prompt sequences scaled to task complexity, knowing when a single well-crafted prompt suffices and when multi-step decomposition is worth the overhead

questions.explanation: Task decomposition determines whether complex work succeeds or fails. Single-prompt approaches for multi-step tasks consistently produce poor results.

questions.text: How often do you specify constraints and edge cases upfront when asking Claude Code to generate code?

choices.label: Never — I let Claude Code figure out the constraints
Sometimes — I mention obvious constraints
Reliably — I list key constraints, edge cases, and error handling expectations
Deliberately — I specify constraints proportional to risk, investing detail in critical paths while keeping low-stakes requests lean

questions.explanation: Upfront constraint specification reduces iteration cycles. Code that misses edge cases often looks correct in the happy path but fails in production.

questions.text: How consistently do you review every diff generated by Claude Code before accepting it?

choices.label: Never — I accept most output without reading it
Sometimes — I skim the diff for obvious issues
Reliably — I read each diff line-by-line before accepting
Maturely — I scale review depth to change risk, scrutinizing critical logic while efficiently approving well-understood mechanical changes

questions.explanation: Diff review is the primary quality gate for AI-generated code. Skipping it leads to subtle bugs accumulating in the codebase.

questions.text: How often do you run tests after each significant change made by Claude Code?

choices.label: Never — I assume the code works
Sometimes — I run tests at the end of a session
Reliably — I run tests after major changes
Deliberately — I choose test scope based on change risk, running targeted tests for focused changes and full suites when changes cross boundaries

questions.explanation: Running tests after each change catches regressions immediately. Waiting until the end of a session makes it harder to identify which change introduced a bug.

questions.text: How often do you verify that AI-generated code handles edge cases correctly rather than only testing the happy path?

choices.label: Never — if the main case works, I move on
Sometimes — I check obvious edge cases
Reliably — I test boundary conditions and error paths
Maturely — I focus edge case testing where failure is costly, balancing thoroughness with the reality that not every code path warrants exhaustive verification

questions.explanation: AI-generated code frequently handles the happy path well but misses edge cases. Systematic edge case verification is what separates reliable from fragile code.

questions.text: How effectively do you use CLAUDE.md files to persist project-level context across sessions?

choices.label: Never — I don't use CLAUDE.md
Sometimes — I have a basic CLAUDE.md but rarely update it
Reliably — I maintain CLAUDE.md with build commands, conventions, and architecture notes
Deliberately — I design CLAUDE.md depth to match project complexity, using layered files where they add value and simple flat configs where they suffice

questions.explanation: CLAUDE.md is the primary mechanism for persistent context. Without it, practitioners repeat the same context-setting work at the start of every session.

questions.text: How well do you manage context window limits across long sessions with Claude Code?

choices.label: Never — I don't think about context limits
Sometimes — I notice when responses degrade but don't know why
Reliably — I recognize context saturation and start fresh sessions when needed
Confidently — I read context signals and apply the right strategy — compact, fresh start, or continuation — based on what the session state actually needs

questions.explanation: Long sessions degrade output quality as the context window fills. Recognizing when to start fresh vs. continue is a key skill that separates competent users from beginners.

questions.text: How consistently do you know when to start a fresh conversation versus continuing an existing one?

choices.label: Never — I always continue in the same session
Sometimes — I start fresh when things feel broken
Reliably — I start fresh when context has drifted or the task scope changes
Deliberately — I make session boundaries a conscious decision, weighing accumulated context value against drift rather than following a rigid rule

questions.explanation: Session management directly affects output quality. Continuing a confused session compounds errors, while starting fresh with good context produces better results.

questions.text: Have you configured CLAUDE.md files, hooks, and permission settings for the projects you work on with Claude Code?

choices.label: Never — I use Claude Code with default settings
Sometimes — I have a basic CLAUDE.md in one or two projects
Reliably — my projects have CLAUDE.md, hooks, and tuned permissions
Maturely — I match environment complexity to project needs, using full tooling for complex projects and lightweight setups where overhead isn't justified

questions.explanation: Environment design is what makes Claude Code effective by default. Without it, every session starts from scratch and every team member reinvents the same configuration.

questions.text: How often do you use hooks to enforce pre-commit checks, linting, or formatting alongside Claude Code?

choices.label: Never — I don't use hooks
Sometimes — I have basic hooks but they aren't integrated with Claude Code
Reliably — I have hooks that catch common issues in AI-generated code
Deliberately — I design hooks that catch high-value issues without creating friction, knowing that over-automated guardrails slow teams down as much as having none

questions.explanation: Hooks provide automated guardrails that catch issues regardless of who generated the code. They are especially valuable for AI-generated code which may not follow project conventions.

questions.text: Have you set up MCP servers to give Claude Code access to external tools, databases, or APIs?

choices.label: Never — I don't know what MCP servers are
Sometimes — I understand MCP conceptually but haven't set one up
Reliably — I have MCP servers configured for key external integrations
Confidently — I set up MCP servers with appropriate security boundaries, choosing which integrations earn the maintenance cost

questions.explanation: MCP servers extend Claude Code's capabilities beyond the local filesystem. Setting them up with proper security is a key Environment Builder skill.

questions.text: How effectively can you run multiple Claude Code agents on parallel tasks?

choices.label: Never — I only use one instance at a time
Sometimes — I've tried running two instances but had conflicts
Reliably — I run parallel agents with worktree isolation on independent tasks
Fluently — I judge when parallelism helps versus when sequential work is simpler, and design isolation strategies matched to actual conflict risk

questions.explanation: Multi-agent parallelism is where AI coding delivers order-of-magnitude gains. Without proper isolation and coordination, parallel agents create merge conflicts and inconsistent output.

questions.text: Have you integrated Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines for automated code generation, review, or testing?

choices.label: Never — I only use Claude Code interactively
Sometimes — I've experimented with non-interactive mode
Reliably — I have Claude Code steps in CI for specific tasks
Maturely — I integrate Claude Code in CI/CD where it adds value, with error handling and monitoring, while knowing which pipeline steps are better served by traditional tooling

questions.explanation: CI/CD integration moves Claude Code from a manual tool to automated infrastructure. This requires non-interactive mode, error handling, and quality monitoring.

questions.text: Do you use headless or non-interactive mode for automated and scripted workflows?

choices.label: Never — I always use Claude Code interactively
Sometimes — I've read about non-interactive mode but haven't used it
Reliably — I use non-interactive mode for batch tasks and scripted workflows
Deliberately — I choose headless mode where automation fits and interactive mode where human judgment matters, building monitoring into the workflows that need it

questions.explanation: Non-interactive mode is the foundation for all automated Claude Code workflows. Understanding its flags and limitations is essential for scaling beyond interactive use.

questions.text: How consistently do you review AI-generated code for security vulnerabilities before accepting it?

choices.label: Never — I don't specifically check for security issues
Sometimes — I check for obvious issues like hardcoded credentials
Reliably — I review for common vulnerability patterns (injection, auth, data exposure)
Maturely — I combine manual review with automated scanning, focusing depth on attack-surface code and trusting well-tested internal paths

questions.explanation: AI-generated code can introduce security vulnerabilities that are syntactically correct but insecure. Security review must be explicit, not assumed.

questions.text: Is there an audit trail for AI-generated changes in your projects — can you trace which code was AI-generated and what prompts produced it?

choices.label: Never — I have no way to distinguish AI-generated code
Sometimes — I use commit messages to note AI involvement
Reliably — I have a consistent labeling practice for AI-generated commits
Deliberately — I maintain traceability proportional to compliance needs, with full audit trails where required and lightweight labeling where they suffice

questions.explanation: As AI coding scales, audit trails become essential for compliance, debugging, and understanding how code was produced. Organizations need traceability.

questions.text: Do you have clear policies for what AI agents can and cannot do autonomously in your projects?

choices.label: Never — there are no policies
Sometimes — I have informal rules but nothing documented
Reliably — I have documented permission boundaries and escalation rules
Maturely — I design governance that scales with autonomy level, with strict boundaries for high-risk actions and lighter touch where the blast radius is small

questions.explanation: Explicit policies prevent unauthorized actions by AI agents. Without them, the boundary between helpful automation and dangerous autonomy is unclear.

questions.text: You ask Claude Code to implement a new API endpoint, and the generated code compiles and returns correct data for your test case. What should you do next?

choices.label: Accept the code — it works
Read through the diff quickly to check for obvious issues
Review the diff line-by-line, run the test suite, and check edge cases
Review the diff, run tests, check edge cases, verify error handling, and review for security vulnerabilities

questions.explanation: A single passing test case is insufficient verification. API endpoints need review for security, error handling, edge cases, and compliance with project conventions.

questions.text: You're implementing a feature that requires changes to the database schema, API layer, and frontend. How do you approach this with Claude Code?

choices.label: Write one prompt describing the entire feature and let Claude Code handle everything
Write separate prompts for each layer but don't specify dependencies between them
Decompose into sequential prompts (schema first, then API, then frontend) with verification after each step
Plan a prompt sequence with explicit inputs/outputs per step, verify each step, and provide the previous step's results as context for the next

questions.explanation: Multi-layer features require ordered decomposition. Each layer depends on the previous one, so verification between steps prevents cascading errors.

questions.text: Your Claude Code session has been running for 45 minutes. Responses are becoming less relevant and occasionally reference files that don't exist. What do you do?

choices.label: Keep going and rephrase your prompts more clearly
Use /clear to reset the conversation and continue
Start a fresh session and re-provide the essential context for your current task
Start a fresh session, scope it narrowly to your remaining task, and provide focused context from CLAUDE.md

questions.explanation: Hallucinating file names is a strong signal of context window saturation. Starting fresh with focused context produces better results than fighting a degraded session.

questions.text: A new team member joins your project and needs to use Claude Code. Your project has no CLAUDE.md or environment configuration. What's the most impactful thing you can do?

choices.label: Tell them to read the project README and figure out Claude Code on their own
Send them a list of useful prompts for the project
Create a CLAUDE.md with build commands, conventions, and architecture overview
Set up a complete environment: layered CLAUDE.md files, hooks, permission config, and MCP servers — so they're productive on day one

questions.explanation: Environment design multiplies productivity across the team. A well-configured project makes every team member effective with Claude Code immediately.

questions.text: You need to refactor 20 modules to use a new logging library. How do you approach this with Claude Code?

choices.label: Ask Claude Code to refactor all 20 modules in a single prompt
Refactor each module one at a time in the same session
Refactor a few modules manually to establish the pattern, then use Claude Code for the rest in batches
Define the pattern in CLAUDE.md, run parallel agents on isolated worktrees — each handling a batch of modules — then merge and verify

questions.explanation: Large-scale refactoring is a prime candidate for multi-agent parallel execution. Worktree isolation prevents conflicts, and a clear pattern definition ensures consistency.

questions.text: Claude Code generates a function that works correctly but uses a deprecated API method. What should you do?

choices.label: Accept it — it works and deprecated doesn't mean removed
Accept it and create a ticket to update later
Ask Claude Code to rewrite using the current API, specifying the correct method
Ask Claude Code to rewrite using the current API, then update CLAUDE.md to note the deprecated methods to avoid

questions.explanation: Fixing the issue and updating CLAUDE.md prevents the same problem from recurring. This is the Environment Builder mindset: solve once, benefit forever.

questions.text: You're designing a CI pipeline that uses Claude Code to automatically generate test cases for new pull requests. What's the most important consideration?

choices.label: Making sure the pipeline runs fast
Ensuring the generated tests actually compile and run
Adding verification that generated tests are meaningful (not just passing trivially) and handling pipeline failures gracefully
Verification of test quality, failure handling, cost monitoring, security review of generated code, and human review gates before merge

questions.explanation: Automated code generation in CI requires multiple layers of verification. Without quality monitoring, trivially passing tests provide false confidence.

questions.text: Your automated Claude Code workflow has been running in CI for two weeks. You notice that the quality of generated code reviews has gradually declined. What's likely happening?

choices.label: The model is getting worse — time to switch tools
The prompts are probably outdated and need refreshing
The codebase has evolved and the context provided to the agent no longer reflects current patterns and conventions
This is quality drift — the environment config, CLAUDE.md, and prompt templates need updating to match codebase evolution, and you need ongoing monitoring metrics

questions.explanation: Quality drift in automated workflows is caused by context becoming stale as the codebase evolves. Monitoring metrics and regular config updates are essential for sustained quality.

questions.text: You're asked to design an AI coding governance framework for a team of 15 engineers. What's your starting point?

choices.label: Ban AI coding tools until you can evaluate them fully
Let everyone use Claude Code freely and address issues as they come up
Define review requirements for AI-generated code and set up basic permission boundaries
Design a phased framework covering permissions, review processes, audit trails, escalation paths, training, and metrics — starting with low-risk use cases

questions.explanation: Governance frameworks must balance safety with productivity. Starting with low-risk use cases and expanding gradually is more effective than either blanket bans or unrestricted access.

questions.text: Claude Code produces a diff that adds 200 lines of code. You're in a hurry. What's the minimum responsible action?

choices.label: Accept it — you can review later
Skim the diff for obvious problems and accept
Read every line of the diff and run the test suite before accepting
Read every line, run tests, check for security issues, and if truly time-pressed, commit to a review branch rather than merging directly

questions.explanation: Time pressure is the most common reason verification is skipped. Using a review branch preserves urgency while maintaining quality gates.

questions.text: What is a 'context window' in the context of Claude Code?

choices.label: The terminal window where Claude Code runs
The maximum number of files Claude Code can open at once
The maximum amount of text (measured in tokens) the model can process in a single interaction
The maximum amount of text the model can process, including all conversation history, file contents, tool outputs, and system instructions

questions.explanation: The context window includes everything in the conversation — not just your prompt but also system instructions, file contents, and tool outputs. Understanding this is fundamental to managing long sessions.

questions.text: What is CLAUDE.md?

choices.label: The documentation for Claude Code itself
A log file that records Claude Code interactions
A markdown file that provides instructions to Claude Code when placed in a project directory
A layered configuration system where project-root and directory-level CLAUDE.md files provide persistent context, conventions, build commands, and behavioral instructions across all sessions

questions.explanation: CLAUDE.md is a persistent configuration mechanism that can be layered (root-level, directory-level) to provide context at different scopes. It is the primary tool for environment design.

questions.text: What is MCP (Model Context Protocol)?

choices.label: A proprietary API for connecting to Anthropic's servers
A protocol for encrypting Claude Code communications
An open protocol for connecting AI models to external tools and data sources
An open protocol with standardized server interfaces that lets Claude Code connect to databases, APIs, file systems, and other external tools — extensible and configurable per project

questions.explanation: MCP is the standard way to extend Claude Code's capabilities. Understanding it as an open, extensible protocol (not a proprietary API) is important for designing integrations.

questions.text: What is a 'verification loop' in AI-assisted development?

choices.label: The process of verifying your Anthropic account credentials
Running a linter after generating code
A practice of reviewing and testing AI-generated output before accepting it
A systematic cycle of reviewing diffs, running tests, checking edge cases, and validating behavior before accepting — repeated after every significant change

questions.explanation: A verification loop is not just running tests — it is a systematic practice that includes diff review, test execution, edge case checking, and behavioral validation after each change.

questions.text: What is 'non-interactive mode' (also called 'headless mode') in Claude Code?

choices.label: Running Claude Code with the screen turned off
Using Claude Code without typing — voice commands only
Running Claude Code without human interaction, using flags like --print, for scripted workflows
Running Claude Code without a terminal UI, using flags like --print or piped input, enabling CI/CD integration, batch processing, and automated workflows with programmatic control

questions.explanation: Non-interactive mode is the foundation for all automated Claude Code usage. Understanding the available flags and input methods is essential for CI/CD integration.

questions.text: What is a 'hook' in the context of Claude Code?

choices.label: A way to connect Claude Code to the internet
A git pre-commit hook only
A user-configured shell command that runs in response to Claude Code events
A user-configured shell command triggered by specific Claude Code events (tool calls, notifications) that can enforce standards, run checks, and automate quality gates

questions.explanation: Hooks in Claude Code are broader than git hooks — they respond to Claude Code-specific events like tool calls. This makes them powerful for enforcing standards during AI-assisted development.

questions.text: What is 'worktree isolation' and why is it important for multi-agent workflows?

choices.label: It means each developer has their own git branch
It means running Claude Code in a Docker container
Using git worktrees so each agent has its own copy of the repository to avoid conflicts

Using git worktrees to give each parallel agent an isolated checkout of the repository, preventing merge conflicts, enabling independent file edits, and allowing clean merges after parallel completion

questions.explanation: Worktree isolation is a critical technique for multi-agent workflows. Without it, parallel agents editing the same files cause merge conflicts and corrupted state.

questions.text: What is 'governance' in the context of AI-generated code?

choices.label: Government regulations about AI
A code review process
Policies and processes that ensure AI-generated code meets quality and compliance standards
An organizational framework of policies, review processes, audit trails, permission boundaries, and controls ensuring AI-generated code meets quality, security, and compliance standards at scale

questions.explanation: Governance goes far beyond code review. It encompasses the full organizational framework for ensuring AI-generated code is safe, compliant, and auditable.

questions.text: What is 'task decomposition' in the context of prompting Claude Code?

choices.label: Deleting completed tasks from a to-do list
Splitting a large prompt into shorter sentences
Breaking a complex task into smaller, sequential prompts that Claude Code can handle individually
Breaking complex work into well-scoped, ordered prompts with explicit inputs, outputs, dependencies, and verification checkpoints between each step

questions.explanation: Task decomposition is not just splitting text — it requires understanding dependencies, defining verification points, and scoping each step so Claude Code has sufficient context.

questions.text: What is a 'subagent' in Claude Code?

choices.label: A junior developer using Claude Code
A Claude Code plugin
A Claude Code instance spawned by another agent to handle a subtask
A Claude Code instance spawned by another agent to handle a subtask, operating with its own context window, task scope, and tool permissions

questions.explanation: Subagents enable hierarchical task decomposition where a parent agent coordinates multiple child agents. Each subagent has its own context window, preventing context overload.

questions.text: When starting a new Claude Code session, how often do you provide project context before making your first request?

choices.label: Rarely — I jump straight to my task
Occasionally — when I remember or the task is complex
Frequently — I reference CLAUDE.md and key files before starting
Consistently — I ensure context is set (via CLAUDE.md or explicit prompts) before every session

questions.explanation: Starting sessions without context is the most common Operator-level behavior. It leads to generic output that doesn't match project conventions.

questions.text: When Claude Code generates code that doesn't work, how do you typically respond?

choices.label: Repeat the same prompt, hoping for a different result
Rephrase the prompt slightly and try again
Analyze the error, provide the error message as context, and ask for a targeted fix
Diagnose the root cause, provide error context and analysis, specify what specifically needs to change, and verify the fix

questions.explanation: Repeating the same prompt is the classic Operator failure mode. Effective error recovery requires providing diagnostic context and specifying the expected behavior.

questions.text: How do you handle the situation when you realize the code Claude Code generated has been silently introducing a subtle pattern violation across multiple files?

choices.label: Fix each file manually as I notice issues
Ask Claude Code to fix the files I've noticed and hope for the best
Identify the pattern violation, fix affected files, and add the convention to my prompts going forward
Fix affected files, document the convention in CLAUDE.md so it persists, and add a hook or linting rule to catch future violations automatically

questions.explanation: The Environment Builder response is to fix the issue AND prevent recurrence by updating CLAUDE.md and adding automated checks. This is the 'solve once' mindset.

questions.text: When Claude Code suggests a change you don't fully understand, what do you typically do?

choices.label: Accept it if it passes basic checks
Reject it and try a different approach
Ask Claude Code to explain the change and its reasoning before deciding
Ask for explanation, verify the reasoning independently, check edge cases, and only accept when you fully understand the implications

questions.explanation: Accepting code you don't understand is a major risk. Even if it works now, you cannot maintain or debug code you didn't verify understanding of.

questions.text: How do you approach using Claude Code for tasks in an unfamiliar codebase?

choices.label: Dive in and ask Claude Code to make changes directly
Ask Claude Code to explain the codebase structure first, then make changes
Explore the codebase yourself, set context about architecture and conventions, then make focused requests
Explore the codebase, read existing CLAUDE.md or create one, set context about architecture, and use small exploratory prompts before making changes

questions.explanation: Working in unfamiliar codebases requires extra context-setting. Diving in without understanding the architecture leads to changes that violate existing patterns.

questions.text: When working on a multi-file change, how do you ensure Claude Code maintains consistency across files?

choices.label: I don't — I handle each file independently
I mention all affected files in my prompt
I provide the interface/contract first, then implement each file referencing the shared contract
I define the contract in context, implement sequentially with verification, and cross-reference outputs to ensure consistency across all files

questions.explanation: Multi-file consistency requires explicit contract definition and sequential verification. Without this, files drift apart and integration fails.

questions.text: How do you handle permission prompts in Claude Code — the tool asking for approval before executing commands or editing files?

choices.label: I always click 'allow all' to avoid interruptions
I approve most things without reading carefully
I read each permission prompt and approve or deny based on the specific action
I've configured permission allowlists for safe operations and review only non-allowlisted actions — balancing safety with productivity

questions.explanation: Permission management is a balance. Clicking 'allow all' bypasses safety guardrails, while reviewing every prompt is slow. Allowlists for safe operations are the mature approach.

questions.text: When an automated Claude Code workflow fails in CI, how do you investigate?

choices.label: Re-run the pipeline and hope it works
Check the error log and adjust the prompt
Review the full agent output, identify the failure point, and fix the prompt or environment configuration
Review agent output, check if the failure is prompt-related or environment-related, fix the root cause, add monitoring for this failure pattern, and update retry logic if needed

questions.explanation: CI failures in automated workflows require systematic debugging. Distinguishing prompt issues from environment issues is key, and adding monitoring prevents recurrence.

questions.text: How do you approach mentoring a colleague who is new to Claude Code?

choices.label: Share a few useful prompts and let them figure it out
Show them the basics and recommend they read the documentation
Set up the project environment for them and teach verification discipline and task decomposition
Assess their current level, provide a structured learning path from Operator through their target level, configure the environment, and pair on real tasks with feedback

questions.explanation: Effective mentoring follows the competence framework. Assessing current level and providing structured progression is far more effective than ad-hoc tips.
